section.title: Building the Temple
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 5 gathered timber, stone, and labor from Israel and from Hiram's Tyre; chapter 6 narrates the actual construction of the temple itself, four hundred and eighty years after the exodus, in exact and deliberate measurements. The chapter describes the main hall, the side chambers, the silent, quarry-cut stones, and, at its very center, an unexpected word from the LORD interrupting the architectural detail to make Solomon's obedience, not merely his building project, the true condition of God's dwelling among His people. The chapter then returns to the interior, the cedar paneling, the gold overlay, the inner sanctuary called the Most Holy Place, the carved cherubim, and the doors, closing with the exact span of seven years the whole work required.
section.thesis: Solomon builds a magnificent house of cedar, gold, and carved cherubim for the Name of the LORD, exactly to specification, yet at the chapter's center the LORD Himself makes plain that His dwelling among His people was never a matter of architecture alone but of covenant obedience, so that the temple's beauty always pointed beyond itself to the greater and final dwelling of God with man in Christ.
section.frameNote: Six beats: the temple's structure begun, verses 1 to 4; the side chambers and quarry-cut construction, verses 5 to 10; the LORD's conditional word to Solomon at the chapter's center, verses 11 to 13; the cedar and gold interior with the inner sanctuary, verses 14 to 22; the carved cherubim, verses 23 to 30; and the doors, courtyard, and the seven-year timeline, verses 31 to 38.

units.label: The temple's structure begun
units.note: four hundred and eighty years after the exodus

verses.text: In the four hundred and eightieth year after the Israelites had come out of the land of Egypt, in the fourth year of Solomon's reign over Israel, in the month of Ziv, the second month, he began to build the {{bayit|house}} of the LORD.
The house that King Solomon built for the LORD was sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high.
The portico at the front of the main hall of the temple was twenty cubits long, extending across the width of the temple and projecting out ten cubits in front of the temple.
He also had narrow windows framed high in the temple.

units.label: The side chambers and quarry-cut construction
units.note: no hammer heard in the house

verses.text: Against the walls of the temple and the {{debir|inner sanctuary}}, Solomon built a chambered structure around the temple, in which he constructed the side rooms.

The bottom floor was five cubits wide, the middle floor six cubits, and the third floor seven cubits. He also placed offset ledges around the outside of the temple, so that nothing would be inserted into its walls.
The temple was constructed using finished stones cut at the quarry, so that no hammer or chisel or any other iron tool was heard in the temple while it was being built.
The entrance to the bottom floor was on the south side of the temple. A stairway led up to the middle level, and from there to the third floor.
So Solomon built the temple and finished it, roofing it with beams and planks of cedar.
He built chambers all along the temple, each five cubits high and attached to the temple with beams of cedar.

units.label: The LORD's word to Solomon
units.note: if you walk in My statutes

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to Solomon, saying:

“As for this temple you are building, if you walk in My statutes, carry out My ordinances, and keep all My commandments by walking in them, I will fulfill through you the promise I made to your father David.
And I will dwell among the Israelites and will not abandon My people Israel.”

units.label: Cedar, gold, and the inner sanctuary
units.note: not a stone could be seen

verses.text: So Solomon built the temple and finished it.
He lined the interior walls with cedar paneling from the floor of the temple to the ceiling, and he covered the floor with cypress boards.
He partitioned off the twenty cubits at the rear of the temple with cedar boards from floor to ceiling to form within the temple an {{debir|inner sanctuary}}, the Most Holy Place.
And the main hall in front of this room was forty cubits long.
The cedar paneling inside the temple was carved with gourds and open flowers. Everything was cedar; not a stone could be seen.
Solomon also prepared the inner sanctuary within the temple to set the ark of the {{berit|covenant}} of the LORD there.
The inner sanctuary was twenty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and twenty cubits high. He overlaid the inside with pure {{zahav|gold}}, and he also overlaid the altar of cedar.
So Solomon overlaid the inside of the temple with pure gold, and he extended gold chains across the front of the inner sanctuary, which was overlaid with gold.
So he overlaid with gold the whole interior of the temple, until everything was completely finished. He also overlaid with gold the entire altar that belonged to the inner sanctuary.
In the inner sanctuary he made two {{keruvim|cherubim}}, each ten cubits high, out of olive wood.
One wing of the first cherub was five cubits long, and the other wing was five cubits long as well. So the full wingspan was ten cubits.
The second cherub also measured ten cubits; both cherubim had the same size and shape,
and the height of each cherub was ten cubits.

And he placed the cherubim inside the innermost room of the temple. Since their wings were spread out, the wing of the first cherub touched one wall, while the wing of the second cherub touched the other wall, and in the middle of the room their wingtips touched.
He also overlaid the cherubim with gold.
Then he carved the walls all around the temple, in both the inner and outer sanctuaries, with carved engravings of cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers.
And he overlaid the temple floor with gold in both the inner and outer sanctuaries.

units.label: The doors, courtyard, and the seven years

verses.text: For the entrance to the inner sanctuary, Solomon constructed doors of olive wood with five-sided doorposts.
The double doors were made of olive wood, and he carved into them cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers and overlaid the cherubim and palm trees with hammered gold.
In the same way he made four-sided doorposts of olive wood for the sanctuary entrance.
The two doors were made of cypress wood, and each had two folding panels.
He carved into them cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers, and he overlaid them with gold hammered evenly over the carvings.
Solomon built the inner courtyard with three rows of dressed stone and one row of trimmed cedar beams.
The foundation of the house of the LORD was laid in the fourth year of Solomon's reign, in the month of Ziv.
In the eleventh year, in the month of Bul, the eighth month, the temple was finished in every detail and according to every specification. So he built the temple in seven years.

bayit.gloss: House. The same ordinary word for a family home is used for the temple throughout this chapter, underscoring that this structure is meant as God's own dwelling, a house where the LORD lives among His people, not merely a national shrine or monument.

debir.gloss: Inner sanctuary, the innermost room of the temple, also called the Most Holy Place, verse 16. Only twenty cubits square yet overlaid entirely in gold, the debir housed the ark of the covenant and the cherubim, the place where the LORD's presence was most concentrated among His people.

berit.gloss: Covenant. The ark of the berit, the covenant, rests in the inner sanctuary, verse 19, the physical reminder that the temple exists because of, and to house the tokens of, the LORD's covenant relationship with Israel, not as an independent monument to human achievement.

keruvim.gloss: Cherubim, the plural of keruv, angelic guardian figures. Carved throughout the temple and towering ten cubits high in the inner sanctuary, they recall the cherubim who guarded Eden after the fall, Genesis 3:24, and the cherubim woven into the tabernacle's veil, marking the inner sanctuary as a guarded, holy meeting place with God.

zahav.gloss: Gold. The repeated overlaying of the temple's interior with pure zahav, verses 20 to 22 and 28 to 30, expresses the surpassing worth and purity fitting for the LORD's dwelling place, so that not a stone could be seen, only cedar and gold, verse 18.

basic.context: Four hundred and eighty years after the exodus, in the fourth year of his reign, Solomon begins building the temple using the timber, stone, and labor prepared in chapter 5. The chapter carefully records the temple's dimensions, its side chambers, and the remarkable detail that no hammer, chisel, or iron tool was heard at the building site because the stones arrived already finished from the quarry. Right at the chapter's center, the LORD interrupts the architectural description with a word to Solomon, making the temple's true significance conditional not on its size or beauty but on Solomon's obedience to God's statutes and commandments. The chapter then returns to the interior, cedar paneling, gold overlay throughout, a windowless inner sanctuary called the Most Holy Place housing towering carved cherubim, and closes by noting the whole project took seven years. The key questions this chapter raises are why God interrupts a building report with a covenant word, what all this cedar, gold, and cherubim symbolism is meant to communicate, and how a structure this magnificent could ever be more than mere architecture.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative with an unusually technical, architectural style; it is not a design manual for churches today, nor are its exact measurements meant to be mystically decoded detail by detail. Its enduring message is carried by the chapter's shape as a whole: careful, costly beauty built for the LORD, punctuated at its very center by God's own insistence that His presence among His people has always depended on covenant obedience, not on buildings, however magnificent.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter calls the temple a bayit, a house, the same word used for an ordinary home, insisting this building is truly God's dwelling. Its innermost room is the debir, the inner sanctuary or Most Holy Place, housing the ark of the berit, the covenant, guarded by towering keruvim, cherubim, and every surface within is overlaid with zahav, gold, so that not a stone could be seen.
basic.typology: The temple as God's dwelling place among His people, guarded at its center by cherubim and housing the ark of the covenant, points forward to Jesus, who is Himself the true temple, the place where God dwells bodily among humanity, and ultimately to the new creation, where God's dwelling is with man and no temple is needed at all because the Lord God and the Lamb are its temple.
basic.christ: At the center of the temple's construction the LORD promises to dwell among the Israelites if Solomon walks in His statutes, verses 12 to 13, exactly the promise Jesus fulfills perfectly and permanently. Jesus said, 'Destroy this temple, and I will raise it up in three days,' speaking of the temple of His body, John 2:19-21, showing that He is the reality the gold and cedar could only picture. Where Solomon's temple depended on continual, uncertain human obedience, Christ's perfect obedience secures forever what Solomon's temple could only symbolize, God truly dwelling with His people.
basic.god: God is a God who delights in beauty and detail, commanding cedar, gold, and carved cherubim for His house, yet He refuses to let a building, however glorious, become a substitute for covenant faithfulness, interrupting the very report of construction to remind Solomon that His presence is conditioned on obedience. He is also the God who has always desired to dwell among His people, from Eden's cherubim-guarded gate to the temple's cherubim-guarded sanctuary, and ultimately in the incarnate Christ and the new creation.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 25 to 26 describes the tabernacle's ark, cherubim, and gold overlay, the pattern the temple now expands in permanent form. Second Samuel 7 records God's original covenant promise to David that grounds the temple's whole purpose. John 2 records Jesus identifying His own body as the true temple. Revelation 21 shows the final fulfillment, a new creation where God Himself is the temple and dwells openly with His people.
basic.oneStory: This chapter narrates the construction of the most significant building in Israel's history, the place where the LORD would dwell among His people as He had among the tabernacle's curtains in the wilderness. Yet God's own words at the chapter's center refuse to let the building itself be the whole story, insisting that His presence depends on obedience. The whole passage becomes one more chapter in the long story of God dwelling with His people, moving from tabernacle to temple to the incarnate Christ, the true temple, and finally to the new creation, where God dwells with man forever without any temple at all, because He and the Lamb are its temple.

context.literary: The chapter is framed by two chronological notices, the fourth year of Solomon's reign in verse 1 and the eleventh year in verse 38, bracketing the entire building account. Within that frame, precise architectural description, verses 1 to 10 and 14 to 36, is interrupted at its structural center by direct divine speech, verses 11 to 13, a placement that forces the reader to weigh the whole building project against the standard of covenant obedience rather than treating the chapter as pure architecture.
context.historical: The temple's basic proportions, roughly ninety feet long, thirty feet wide, and forty-five feet high, echo the tabernacle's measurements at double scale, situating it within Israel's existing worship tradition rather than inventing something wholly new. Building with pre-cut quarry stone avoided the ritual impurity iron tools were thought to bring to an altar area, Exodus 20:25, and lining a stone building entirely in cedar and gold reflects known ancient Near Eastern temple-building practices where interior wood paneling and precious metal overlay signified a deity's honored dwelling.
context.canonical: This chapter stands at the center of the temple narrative that runs from chapters 5 through 8, and its architecture deliberately expands the tabernacle's pattern, ark, cherubim, gold, inner sanctuary, first given at Sinai, Exodus 25 to 26. The LORD's conditional word in verses 11 to 13 anticipates the covenant warnings later repeated to Solomon in 1 Kings 9:6-9 and explains, in advance, why the temple would eventually be destroyed when that obedience failed, 2 Kings 25:9.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record, in careful and reverent detail, the construction of the temple as the fulfillment of God's promise to David, while placing at its very structural center God's own insistence that His dwelling among His people was always conditioned on covenant obedience rather than on the building's beauty or scale alone.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific ancient building with specific measurements and materials; it does not prescribe architectural style or material for worship spaces today. What endures as timeless truth is that God delights in offering our best, costly excellence, in His worship, and that no structure, however magnificent, replaces the necessity of walking in obedience if His presence and blessing are to remain.

debates.question: Why does the LORD's word in verses 11 to 13 interrupt the architectural description right at this point in the chapter?

views.label: A deliberate structural signal placed at the temple's literal center
views.case: The word arrives precisely as Solomon completes the temple's basic shell, verses 9-10, and before its costly interior is described, verses 14 onward, positioning obedience, not craftsmanship, as the true center of the whole building enterprise; its placement is the narrator's way of making a theological point through structure itself.

views.label: A necessary clarification following the earlier promise
views.case: Since 2 Samuel 7 already promised David an everlasting house, the LORD inserts this word to prevent Solomon, or later readers, from assuming the physical temple itself guarantees God's presence unconditionally; the interruption functions less as literary art and more as a plain, timely theological correction.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree on the substance: God's presence and covenant blessing were never automatic or guaranteed by architecture alone but remained tied to obedience, a truth Solomon's own later unfaithfulness, chapter 11, and the temple's eventual destruction would tragically confirm.

study.originalLanguages: The whole chapter calls this building a bayit, house, the ordinary Hebrew word for a home, insisting God truly dwells here rather than merely being represented by a monument. Its innermost chamber is named the debir, inner sanctuary or Most Holy Place, verse 16, the very room built to hold the ark of the berit, the covenant, verse 19, guarded by towering keruvim, cherubim, whose wings stretch wall to wall. And nearly every surface within is overlaid in zahav, gold, until, as verse 18 says, not a stone could be seen, a visual sermon in itself: the LORD's dwelling place is to be nothing but costly, covenant-marked, glory-laden beauty.

typology.type: The temple as the place of God's dwelling among His covenant people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Word made flesh who tabernacled among us, and His own body identified as the true temple, John 1:14, John 2:19-21.

typology.type: The cherubim guarding the inner sanctuary that houses the ark of the covenant
typology.fulfillment: Christ as the true mercy seat and meeting place between God and man, Romans 3:25, Hebrews 9:11-12.

typology.type: A house built and overlaid entirely for the glory and worth of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The new creation, where God's dwelling is with man and the Lord God and the Lamb are its temple, Revelation 21:3, Revelation 21:22.

study.christ: At the very center of the building account, God promises to dwell among the Israelites if Solomon keeps His commandments, verses 12 to 13, a conditional promise that Israel's history shows was ultimately not kept. Jesus points to Himself as the reality this whole structure could only anticipate, declaring that the temple of His body would be destroyed and raised again in three days, John 2:19-21, and John explicitly says the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, John 1:14, using the very vocabulary of God's dwelling among His people. Where Solomon's temple depended on fragile, repeated human obedience, Christ's perfect obedience secures forever, unconditionally, what the temple's gold and cedar could only symbolize.
study.god: God delights in costly beauty offered to Him, commanding cedar, gold, and carved cherubim throughout His house, yet He refuses to let even the most magnificent structure substitute for covenant obedience, interrupting the very report of construction to say so plainly. He is the God who has always desired to dwell with His people, from the cherubim guarding Eden's gate after the fall to the cherubim guarding the temple's inner sanctuary, and He remains faithful to that desire even when His people's obedience fails, ultimately dwelling among us Himself in Christ.

crossReferences.note: The tabernacle's ark and cherubim, the pattern the temple's inner sanctuary now expands into permanent, gold-covered form.
The LORD's original covenant promise to David that the temple now begins to fulfill.
Jesus identifies the temple of His own body as the true and final reality the physical temple pictured.
The new creation where God dwells openly with His people and He and the Lamb are its temple, needing no other.

study.oneStory: This chapter narrates the construction of the most significant building in Israel's history, a costly, gold-covered house built so that the LORD might dwell among His people as He had among the tabernacle's curtains in the wilderness. Yet God's own interrupting word insists that this dwelling was always conditioned on covenant obedience, not architecture alone, a truth painfully proven when the temple was later destroyed. The whole passage is one more chapter in the long story of God dwelling with His people, moving from tabernacle to temple to the incarnate Christ, the true and greater temple, and finally to the new creation, where God dwells with man forever and no temple remains necessary, because He and the Lamb are its temple.

quiz.q: What is remarkable about how the temple's stones were prepared, verse 7?

options.text: The stones were finished at the quarry, so that no hammer, chisel, or iron tool was heard at the temple site during construction.
options.note: Verse 7 states this explicitly as a distinctive feature of the building process.

options.text: The stones were extremely large but required significant on-site cutting and shaping.
options.note: The stones were indeed large and costly, verse 17 in chapter 5, but the point of verse 7 is precisely that no on-site cutting tools were heard.

options.text: The temple was built entirely of wood, with no stone used at all.
options.note: Stone forms the temple's structure, verse 7; cedar covers the interior, but the building itself is stone.

options.text: The construction process was loud and disruptive to the surrounding area.
options.note: The text specifically emphasizes the silence of construction due to pre-quarried stones.

quiz.q: What does the LORD's word to Solomon in verses 11 to 13 make the true condition of God's dwelling among His people?

options.text: Not the temple's size or beauty, but Solomon walking in God's statutes, keeping His ordinances and commandments.
options.note: Verses 12-13 state this condition directly, right at the chapter's structural center.

options.text: The completion of the temple within a set number of years.
options.note: The temple does take seven years, verse 38, but the condition God states is obedience, not a construction timeline.

options.text: The amount of gold used to overlay the interior.
options.note: Gold overlay is described at length later in the chapter, but it is not named as the condition for God's presence.

options.text: There was no condition attached to God's presence at all.
options.note: Verses 12-13 explicitly attach a conditional promise tied to obedience.

quiz.q: Why is it significant that verse 18 says 'not a stone could be seen' inside the temple?

options.text: It shows that the entire visible interior was covered in cedar and gold, expressing the costly beauty and honor fitting for the LORD's dwelling place.
options.note: This detail underscores the lavish, deliberate beauty of the temple's interior, all stone hidden beneath cedar and gold.

options.text: It shows that the temple was actually built without any stone in its structure.
options.note: Stone forms the temple's actual structure, verse 7; this verse describes only the interior paneling covering that stone.

options.text: It shows that the builders ran out of stone during construction.
options.note: There is no indication of a shortage; the covering was a deliberate design choice for beauty.

options.text: It shows that the temple's interior was left entirely bare and unfinished.
options.note: The text describes an elaborately finished interior, fully paneled in carved cedar and overlaid with gold.

quiz.q: What did Solomon set inside the inner sanctuary, the Most Holy Place, verse 19?

options.text: The ark of the covenant of the LORD, housed within the gold-overlaid inner sanctuary guarded by two large cherubim.
options.note: Verses 19-23 describe the ark's placement and the accompanying cherubim in the inner sanctuary.

options.text: The royal throne of Solomon himself.
options.note: Solomon's throne is described elsewhere, 1 Kings 10:18-20, but the inner sanctuary specifically housed the ark of the covenant, not the king's throne.

options.text: A collection of Solomon's written proverbs and songs.
options.note: Solomon's proverbs and songs are mentioned in chapter 4, but they are not said to be placed in the inner sanctuary.

options.text: The inner sanctuary was left completely empty.
options.note: The text explicitly says the ark of the covenant was placed there.

quiz.q: How long did it take Solomon to build the temple, and what closing note does the chapter give, verses 37 to 38?

options.text: Seven years, from the foundation laid in the fourth year of his reign to its completion in every detail in the eleventh year.
options.note: Verses 37-38 give this precise timeline and note it was finished according to every specification.

options.text: Just under one year, completed quickly with minimal detail.
options.note: The text specifies a much longer timeline, seven years, with meticulous attention to detail throughout.

options.text: Solomon never actually finished the temple within his lifetime.
options.note: Verse 38 explicitly states the temple was finished in every detail.

options.text: The chapter gives no indication of how long construction took.
options.note: Verses 37-38 explicitly provide the start and completion years.

quiz.q: What do the carved cherubim throughout the temple, verses 23 to 29, recall from earlier in Scripture?

options.text: The cherubim who guarded the way to the tree of life after the fall, and the cherubim woven into the tabernacle's veil and ark, marking the temple as a similarly guarded, holy meeting place with God.
options.note: This connects the temple's cherubim imagery to Genesis 3:24 and Exodus 25-26, a consistent biblical symbol of guarded holiness.

options.text: They recall mainly decorative Phoenician artistic style with no theological connection to earlier Scripture.
options.note: While ancient Near Eastern craftsmanship shaped the artistry, the cherubim motif itself carries deliberate theological continuity from Eden and the tabernacle.

options.text: They represent Solomon's personal coat of arms.
options.note: The cherubim are consistently a biblical symbol tied to God's guarded presence, not a personal royal emblem.

options.text: They have no connection to anything earlier in the Bible.
options.note: The cherubim motif directly echoes Genesis 3:24 and the tabernacle's cherubim, Exodus 25:18-20.

questions: The temple's stones arrived silent and already finished from the quarry. Where might God be asking you to trust preparation done quietly, out of sight, rather than needing to see every step of the process happen visibly before you?

Right at the center of the building report, God tells Solomon that His presence depends on obedience, not architecture. Where are you tempted to trust an outward structure, a routine, a place, a program, rather than a walk of daily obedience with God?

Solomon covered the temple so thoroughly in cedar and gold that not a stone could be seen. What would it look like to offer God your very best in worship this week, rather than whatever is convenient or left over?

The ark of the covenant, God's covenant promise in physical form, rested at the very center of the temple, guarded by towering cherubim. How does knowing Christ is the true meeting place between you and God change how you approach Him today?
It took seven years of careful, unhurried work to finish the temple in every detail. Where do you need patience for something God is building in your life that simply is not finished yet?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes the Hebrew reads four hundred and eightieth year, while the LXX reads four hundred and fortieth; the difference reflects a well-known textual variant in the chronology of the period between the exodus and the temple's construction.
The BSB footnote follows the LXX and Targum reading 'bottom' floor for the entrance's level; the Hebrew text reads 'middle,' another minor textual variant regarding the temple's internal stairway.
The BSB footnote allows 'the altar of cedar' to be rendered simply 'with cedar,' affecting whether this clause describes a distinct wooden altar or the cedar overlay of the inner sanctuary generally.

The BSB footnote offers an alternate rendering, 'made gold chains to draw the curtains,' for the gold chains extended across the front of the inner sanctuary, showing some uncertainty over their precise function.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 6:1-38, all thirty-eight verses included in full given the chapter's detailed measurements. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced elsewhere in this file.
